content.text:
[
  {
    "ruleId": "R10",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "blocking",
    "evidence": "Beat-01's visual is two columns of numbers (concrete) sliding in and lighting up; beat-02 opens with 'One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear' with no scripted morph connecting the number-columns to the arrow representation. Later, beat-03's transition from the meter dial to the symbolic notation is described only as the annotation 'sits on the score readout' rather than the dial visually resolving into a . b. Neither transition shows the fade the rule requires.",
    "suggestion": "Show the paired number columns physically resolve into the arrow pair (each column collapses to a point, a vector is drawn from the origin) before beat-02's arrows appear, and have the a . b annotation crystallize out of the meter dial/readout rather than simply appearing beside it."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R2",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "evidence": "Beat-02's core spoken claim, 'multiply them entry by entry and add,' plays over a visual that only shows arrows appearing and a lilac score bar 'at its current value'; nowhere in the script or page is the entry-wise multiplication or running sum animated. The mechanism that gives the dot product its name is asserted in narration but never rendered as an operation.",
    "suggestion": "Add one worked moment, before the sweep, where a single candidate's coordinates are multiplied against the probe's coordinates on screen (each pair highlighting, its product landing in a running total), so the naming claim has a literal visual referent."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R28",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "evidence": "The dot-product/meter deep structure is taught through exactly one surface instance across all four beats: a probe arrow against four candidates within a single music-taste-vs-song scenario. No second surface-different instance is taught and aligned within the session; the extensions menu only gestures at other domains (search, translation, attention) without teaching them.",
    "suggestion": "Either add a second quick instance reusing the identical multiply-and-add rule in a visibly different surface domain before the aha is named, or record the single-instance choice explicitly as a declared compromise in spine.md so the gap is a disclosed strain rather than a silent one."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R19",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "evidence": "The prompt immediately preceding beat-04's length-problem reveal is 'Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?' This is a retrieval request about already-taught material, not a prediction about the new information about to be revealed (that stretching a candidate inflates its score without changing direction). The letter of a pre-reveal prompt is satisfied, but no forward guess is demanded before this particular reveal.",
    "suggestion": "Before the stretch demonstration, ask something predictive, e.g. 'If we make this candidate twice as long without turning it, what happens to its score?' so the length-problem reveal lands inside a genuine open loop rather than after a pure retrieval exercise."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R3",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "evidence": "The built page renders the sweep readout as a flat key, 'c1 c2 c3 c4 probe 90°' followed by 'c1 0.34 c2 0.94 c3 0.94 c4 0.34,' which reads as a legend indexed by abbreviation rather than labels sitting on the arrows/bars themselves, even though the script's visual intent describes labels attached to objects ('under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout').",
    "suggestion": "Confirm the rendered widget places c1-c4 labels and their live scores directly on or beside each arrow/bar; if the text above reflects the actual layout, move the labels onto the objects and drop the separate key."
  }
]

content.text:
```json
[
  {
    "ruleId": "R10",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "blocking",
    "evidence": "beat-01's concrete stage (two columns of numbers) never visually becomes beat-02's schematic stage (probe/candidate arrows); the arrows simply 'appear' in beat-02 with no morph shown from the number-lists that closed beat-01. The diagram-to-notation fade (arrows -> a·b in beat-03) is shown, but the concrete-to-schematic fade is not.",
    "suggestion": "have the paired number columns from beat-01 visually reshape into the probe/candidate arrows at the start of beat-02 (e.g. each pair of numbers rotates into a vector), so the concrete->schematic step is an on-screen morph, not a cut."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R12",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "blocking",
    "evidence": "beat-02's opening visual has the probe arrow, four candidate arrows, AND already-populated lilac score bars ('sits at its current value') appearing together before the narration has explained what an arrow means or how the score is produced ('Strip it down. A taste is an arrow...' comes after the scores are already visible). The objects' interaction (a computed score) is on screen before either object is individually characterized.",
    "suggestion": "bring in the probe and candidates first with no score bars, let the narration characterize what an arrow/quality is, and only pop the score bars in once multiply-and-add has been stated and the first sweep begins."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R19",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "blocking",
    "evidence": "beat-04's 'Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved' is a major reveal (the crack that motivates the entire breadcrumb) but no prediction is demanded before it; the visual cuts straight to the stretched candidate and its rose-flagged unearned win with no 'what do you think happens if a candidate gets longer' prompt.",
    "suggestion": "insert a short predict-before-reveal beat: freeze the candidate at its original length and ask whether stretching it (without changing direction) would move its score, before showing the stretch."
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "R26",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "evidence": "beat-02 ends with a declarative recap ('The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness') and beat-03 ends with a declarative recap ('the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed') rather than a generative act; only beat-04's close (the walk-home question and breadcrumb) is genuinely generative. The generative element that exists in these sections (the beat-02 prediction) sits mid-section, not at the close, so each section's actual ending is a summary.",
    "suggestion": "close beat-02 and beat-03 on a small generative ask instead of a wrap-up sentence, e.g. beat-02 could end by having the learner name what a negative score would mean for a candidate before beat-03 states it, or beat-03 could end by asking the learner to predict the score for a fifth, unseen candidate direction."
  }
]
```
